text: The fenestrated bipolar forceps grasp the descending sigmoid junction, with visible epipelvic appendages nearby. The surgeon tents up the descending sigmoid junction in the anterior aspect of the abdominal cavity, preparing to pass it to the third robotic arm.

The Tip-up grasper holds the anticipated proximal division point while the surgeon uses a vessel sealer and a fenestrated bipolar in the left hand to locate the divided inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) pedicle, which was ligated high in the operation. The inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) is visible running parallel to the IMA.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to tent the IMA pedicle towards the right lower quadrant. The cyst shifts to grasp the IMA pedicle, pulling it further to the right lower quadrant. The vessel sealer is then used to burn the mesentery in a northern direction, aiming towards the third arm. The older vessel sealer is employed, requiring a double burn on the mesentery.

The surgeon advances along the mesentery towards the mesenteric border of the descending sigmoid junction, using the third robotic arm as a fixed reference point for precise vessel sealing. The surgeon carefully dissects and removes epiploic appendages from the mesenteric border in preparation for an anastomosis.

The surgeon uses a fenestrated bipolar grasper with the left hand to pull the descending mesocolon to the left, while the assistant pulls the divided IMA pedicle to the right lower quadrant. The vessel sealer is aimed north towards the third arm, preparing for the insertion of the anvil.

The vessel sealer is positioned between the right lower quadrant and the IMA pedicle, with the left hand pulling the descending mesocolon in a northern direction. The surgeon uses the fixed third arm as a reference point to accurately aim the vessel sealer, ensuring precise dissection without deviating proximally or distally.

The surgeon administers 2.5 cc's of ICG followed by a 10 cc flush of normal saline, observing the tissue perfusion at the descending sigmoid junction. The epiploic appendages are divided, and the junction is devascularized, with visible perfusion indicating successful blood flow.

The surgeon checks the mesenteric orientation, highlighting the divided inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) and artery (IMA) pedicles. The camera view shows the cut edge of the mesentery, which is traced down to ensure proper alignment. The assistant adjusts the staple to achieve optimal orientation for completing the end-to-end anastomosis.
The surgeon performs hydrodynamic testing using a flexible sigmoidoscope, followed by a final assessment of tissue perfusion with ICG, ensuring optimal perfusion of the surgical site.
